en.extract:
The Mayotte scops owl (Otus mayottensis) is a species of owl in the family Strigidae. It is endemic to the island of Mayotte in the Comoros.


Taxonomy
The Mayotte scops owl has been considered to be conspecific with the Pemba scops owl (O. pembaensis), the Anjouan scops owl (O. capnodes), the rainforest scops owl and the Torotoroka scops owl (O. madagascariensis under O. rutilus), but this species has now been split and the Mayotte scops owl is now regarded as its own species, mainly based on its very different call. The scops owls of Madagascar, Comoros and Seychelles form a clade within the genus Otus with the Oriental scops owl (O. sunia) and are not closely related to the continental African scops owls.


Description
The Mayotte scops owl is very similar to the rainforest scops owl (O. rutilus) with which it was once considered conspecific. The upperparts are plain brown marked with faint light spots while the underparts are paler brown than the upperparts. The facial disk is also brownish with a contrasting darker rim restricted to just the sides and not seen above the eyes. The crown is brown with darker streaks and the flanks are more reddish-brown with short dark streaks. The eyes are yellow. The body length is 20–22 cm (7.9–8.7 in).


Voice
The call of the Mayotte scops owl is a series of 3–11 deep, single hoots.


Distribution and habitat
It is endemic to Maore island in Mayotte, where it is common and widespread. The habitat of the Mayotte scops owl is subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests.


Status
The population of the Mayotte scops owl is thought to be stable as there is no evidence for any decline or of any substantial threats, thus it is categorised as least concern.


References

The '''Mayotte scops owl''' ('''''Otus mayottensis''''') is a species of [[owl]] in the family [[Strigidae]]. It is [[endemic]] to the island of [[Mayotte]] in the Comoros.

==Taxonomy==
The Mayotte scops owl has been considered to be conspecific with the [[Pemba scops owl]] (''O. pembaensis''), the [[Anjouan scops owl]] (''O. capnodes''), the rainforest scops owl and the [[Torotoroka scops owl]] (''O. madagascariensis'' under ''O. rutilus''), but this species has now been split and the Mayotte scops owl is now regarded as its own species, mainly based on its very different call.<ref name = Kemp>{{cite book | last1 = Kemp | first1 = Alan | last2 = Kemp |first2 = Meg | year = 1998 |  title = Birds of Prey of Africa and its Islands | page = 316 | publisher = New Holland | isbn =1-85974-100-2}}</ref><ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" /> The scops owls of [[Madagascar]], Comoros and [[Seychelles]] form a [[clade]] within the genus [[Otus (bird)|Otus]] with the [[Oriental scops owl]] (''O. sunia'') and are not closely related to the continental African scops owls.<ref name = Fuchs/>

==Description==
The Mayotte scops owl is very similar to the [[rainforest scops owl]] (''O. rutilus'') with which it was once considered conspecific. The upperparts are plain brown marked with faint light spots while the underparts are paler brown than the upperparts. The facial disk is also brownish with a contrasting darker rim restricted to just the sides and not seen above the eyes. The crown is brown with darker streaks and the flanks are more reddish-brown with short dark streaks. The eyes are yellow. The body length is {{convert|20|-|22|cm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name = planetofbirds>{{cite web | url = http://www.planetofbirds.com/strigiformes-strigidae-mayotte-scops-owl-otus-mayottensis | title = Mayotte scops owl (Otus mayottensis) | access-date = 26 October 2016 | publisher = Planet of Birds}}</ref>

===Voice===
The call of the Mayotte scops owl is a series of 3–11 deep, single hoots.<ref name = Fuchs>{{cite journal | last1 = Fuchs| first1= Jérôme|display-authors=etal| year = 2008 | title = Tracing the colonization history of the Indian Ocean scops-owls (Strigiformes: Otus) with further insight into the spatio-temporal origin of the Malagasy avifauna | journal = BMC Evolutionary Biology | doi = 10.1186/1471-2148-8-197 | pmid= 18611281| volume=8 | issue= 1| page=197| pmc = 2483963 | bibcode= 2008BMCEE...8..197F| doi-access= free}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
It is endemic to [[Grande-Terre (Mayotte)|Maore]] island in [[Mayotte]],<ref name = Birdlife>{{cite web | url = http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/speciesfactsheet.php?id=31720 | title = Mayotte Scops-owl ''Otus mayottensis'' |access-date=23 October 2016 | publisher = [[Birdlife International]]}}</ref> where it is common and widespread.<ref name = Kemp/> The habitat of the Mayotte scops owl is subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests.<ref name = planetofbirds/>

==Status==
The population of the Mayotte scops owl is thought to be stable as there is no evidence for any decline or of any substantial threats, thus it is categorised as [[least concern]].<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" /><ref name = Birdlife/>
